html:
StarlinePS Enterprises

StarlinePS is not the old NBFC or hidden net-cash stone-trading bargain. The current facts point to a more volatile but potentially higher-torque setup: a tiny Surat diamond and jewelry trader is trying to recapitalize itself into a solar cell/module manufacturing platform just as India’s domestic solar-cell mandate starts to bite.

The legacy business is real but not enough to own the stock by itself. In the Dec. 2025 filing, the company still reported a single segment: trading of diamonds and jewelry. Q3 FY26 revenue was ₹20.19 Cr and PAT was only ₹0.73 Cr; 9M FY26 PAT was ₹3.18 Cr. On the May 18 price of ₹11.4, the stock is not cheap on trailing earnings, with Screener showing a headline P/E near 180x and a market cap around ₹413 Cr before fully reflecting the new dilution.

The variant perception is the capital structure and business pivot. Starline raised ₹20.75 Cr through a rights issue at ₹2, then disclosed full use of that money for working capital, borrowing repayment, and creditor payments. That is not a sign of surplus cash; it is a sign the diamond business needed oxygen. But then management approved a much larger preferential issue: up to 7 Cr equity shares and 48 Cr convertible warrants at ₹6, raising up to ₹330 Cr if all warrants convert. The stated use is not diamonds. It is ₹235 Cr for acquiring a controlling stake in solar cell manufacturing or establishing solar cell/module facilities, ₹15 Cr for business expansion, and ₹80 Cr for general corporate purposes, per the EGM notice. The allotment list shows promoters receiving 21.5 Cr warrants and non-promoters receiving the balance equity/warrants.

That changes the stock. On the old share count, the market cap looks like ₹413 Cr. After the 7 Cr equity allotment, the effective listed equity value is closer to ₹494 Cr at ₹11.4. Fully diluted for all 48 Cr warrants, there would be 91.3 Cr shares and a fully diluted equity value around ₹1,041 Cr, offset by up to ₹330 Cr of gross cash coming into the company. So the question is not whether the diamond business is cheap. It is whether a roughly ₹700 Cr net enterprise value can be justified by a credible solar manufacturing acquisition or buildout.

There is a real macro reason this could work. India’s solar policy is forcing domestic content deeper into the supply chain. From June 1, 2026, government-backed projects must use modules made with cells from ALMM List-II, and India’s module capacity has run far ahead of cell capacity. PV Magazine reported about 210 GW of module capacity versus roughly 27 GW of cell capacity as of end-2025, while recent MNRE updates are expanding List-II cell capacity. That bottleneck can make even smaller cell/module assets strategically valuable if they are ALMM-eligible, financed, and tied to customers.

The bull case is that Starline is becoming a listed solar manufacturing shell at exactly the right policy moment. Promoters bought stock in the open market in 2024-25 at ₹6.38 to ₹9.10, then subscribed to in-the-money warrants at ₹6. If they convert, the company gets enough capital to matter relative to its old size. A concrete acquisition of an operating or near-operating solar cell/module asset could instantly re-label the stock from “illiquid diamond trader” to “domestic solar manufacturing proxy.” With no sell-side coverage, low institutional ownership, and a retail-heavy shareholder base, that re-labeling can move the stock well before earnings appear.

The bear case is equally obvious. The company has no demonstrated solar manufacturing track record, the preferential issue is massively dilutive, the use of proceeds is broad, and the rights issue just funded payables and borrowings. The diamond business has compressed sharply from FY25’s ₹6.58 Cr PAT run-rate to a much weaker FY26 pace. The company’s own rights document projected long receivable cycles and higher working-capital needs. If the solar capital is delayed, poorly deployed, or used for a vague acquisition without ALMM eligibility, the stock should not trade as a solar platform. It should trade as a small, low-margin trading business with governance and dilution risk.

My base view: this is not a clean value stock. It is a high-torque transformation option. The stock can still work after a large move because the relevant future value is not the current diamond P&L, but the optionality of deploying ₹330 Cr into a policy-favored solar asset. But until a specific asset, capacity, technology, customer, and funding plan are disclosed, the position has to be sized like a speculative event-driven microcap, not an earnings compounder.

Bear target: ₹5.00, down 56%. No credible solar transaction, diamond earnings remain around ₹3 to ₹4 Cr, receivables absorb cash, and the market values the company near ₹200 to ₹250 Cr on the enlarged listed share base.

Base target: ₹14.00, up 23%. Warrants remain in the money, the company receives most of the ₹330 Cr, announces a credible but not yet proven solar acquisition/buildout, and the market gives partial credit for the solar platform while still discounting execution.

Bull target: ₹28.00, up 146%. Starline secures an ALMM-relevant solar cell/module asset, converts the warrants, shows real orders or offtake, and the market underwrites ₹75 to ₹100 Cr of medium-term PAT or high-quality EBITDA from the new platform. At that point, a fully diluted equity value of ₹2,500 Cr is possible, which is about ₹28 per share on 91.3 Cr fully diluted shares.
